status.name: Language Hole-Filling G3-12
status.subject: Language
status.replaces:
Current Language hole-filling stages in the PowerPath sequence — see https://timeback-loops-k8.vercel.app/course-sequence

status.hole_filling: This project IS the hole-filling layer for Language G3-12.
status.parent_summary: This Language Hole-Filling course helps students in grades 3–12 close specific gaps in grammar, punctuation, and usage — the same skills measured by the MAP Language Usage test. Rather than putting every student through the same material, a diagnostic first identifies which specific skills a student is missing, and the student is then assigned only the lessons needed to close those gaps, each requiring an 80% mastery score before moving on. Mastery checkpoints include AI-graded rewrite tasks that accept any valid correct answer rather than one memorized phrase, and quizzes draw from large question pools so students can't pass by guessing or memorizing; time to finish varies by student since it depends on how many gaps they have, and success is measured not just by clearing in-course checkpoints but by whether those same skills stop showing up as gaps on a later MAP Language Usage retest.
status.standards_covered: [AI guess — verify] Per grade: the CCSS Language strand (L.n.1-L.n.6) plus selected W.n writing-convention standards — one lesson per standard (G3 = 45 lessons; standards sheet linked in the BrainLift). Not covered: reading comprehension, composition as a taught craft, content-area knowledge — it remediates conventions/usage gaps, it is not a full ELA course. (source: owner BrainLift)
status.passes_test: [AI guess — verify] Instrument: NWEA MAP Language Usage — the diagnostic that places students and the metric the remediation is accountable to move. status.entry_gate: [AI guess — verify] None — diagnostic-placed rather than entry-gated: MAP Language Usage flags the specific standard-level holes, and the student is assigned exactly those lessons. (source: owner BrainLift Q4)
status.xp_hours: [AI guess — verify] Varies with the number of holes: knows-all ~1-2 hrs (mostly testing out), median ~4-8 hrs, knows-nothing in-band ~15-25 hrs (grade-dependent; estimates). ~0.9-1.1 XP/min. (source: owner BrainLift Q5-Q7, re-read 2026-07-22)

status.name: Alpha CCSS Grade Tests
status.subject: Math
status.replaces:
Alpha Standardized K-2 and STAAR G3-8 gates.

Where it lands: Next-year Math mastery gates: Alpha CCSS Grade K-8.
status.parent_summary: Every fall, winter, and spring, students across the country take the NWEA MAP Growth test — the national yardstick for how a child is doing in math. Our old practice tests were built for other exams and didn't match MAP's style or difficulty. The new K–8 math tests fix that: for each grade there are 10 different versions of a test built to look and feel like MAP — same topics, same question styles (dragging, typing answers, clicking on figures, not just multiple choice) — but deliberately a notch harder, because our goal isn't passing, it's the 99th percentile. Because there are 10 versions, a child can't memorize answers; a retake is always a fresh test of the same skills. Tests are untimed, short for the youngest (about 20 questions in kindergarten) and longer by 8th grade (about 42), with read-aloud support through 5th grade. When your child masters the test for their grade, they should be ready to score at the very top of MAP for their age.
status.standards_covered: [AI guess — verify] 100% of CCSS math per grade K-8, weighted by CCSS major-work emphasis and MAP goal areas (which map 1:1 to CCSS domains). Not covered: state standards beyond CCSS (the State Supplements + Tests project carries those), DOK 4, non-math subjects. (source: owner BrainLift)
status.passes_test: [AI guess — verify] NWEA MAP Growth: ~99th-percentile achievement at the student's age grade plus 2X growth (observed RIT growth >= 2x norm-projected). The 90%-cut <-> 99th-percentile link is provisional until the bridge-sample equating on 2025 norms lands — owner tracks it as the project's #1 risk and will not publish the claim before the link. (source: owner BrainLift)
status.entry_gate: [AI guess — verify] Entry: assigned at the student's knowledge grade in the CCSS sequence (age grade may differ). Exit: 90% on any of the grade's 10 parallel forms; retakes rotate to a different form — no student sees the same items twice. (source: owner BrainLift)
status.xp_hours: [AI guess — verify] It is an assessment, not a course: seat time per sitting, untimed with pause/resume — K ~20-30 min (20 items, splittable, TTS on) ramping to G8 ~55-65 min (42 items); no calculator K-7. Knows-all: one sitting; remediation lives in the course via CCSS-tagged miss mapping. Farming near-zero (10 disjoint versions + rotation). (source: owner BrainLift)
status.effective_for: [AI guess — verify] Every Alpha K-8 math student — the universal mastery gate. Highest marginal value for the 230+ students already at MAP's 99th percentile, where DOK-1-heavy instruments have zero discrimination. Named bridge-sample roster pending the TimeBack pull. (source: owner BrainLift)
status.bottleneck: QCing is slow because the audio must be re-done on every test after every minor edit. AT team is working on a fix.
status.notes: The "Alpha Common Core" Math K-8 test banks replacing Alpha Standardized/STAAR gates; lock + deliver Week 2 of the Aug-1 push. QA signal from Worksmart: the 100-For-100 team's QA found WRONG ANSWER KEYS in existing TimeBack tests — worth folding their checks into the CCSS bank QC.

status.name: State Supplements + Tests
status.subject: Math

status.hole_filling: The supplement is built FROM the hole-filling inventory: 115 of 138 lessons linked by sourcedId from the 342-lesson Timeback HF pool (reused, never copied); the 23 authored lessons join the pool for future states. Remediation loop: one unit per standard, so a missed standard on Form A maps directly to its unit — re-assign the unit, retest on Form B/C. No separate HF course needed.
status.supplements: This is the supplement layer itself. Needs: the national grade-level course as prerequisite (or concurrent) — never re-teaches it. Contains: one unit per measured gap standard — TX 20, FL 19, MA 3, AZ 4, CA 0. Which: MATH-G3-tx/fl/ma/az-supplement (AlphaLearn org), each paired with Test Forms A/B/C; expands PK-12 × 51 jurisdictions by lineage class.
status.parent_summary: In some states, the regular “national” math course leaves out a few things the state test still expects — like personal finance in Texas or tricky time and data questions in Florida. The Math State Supplements are short add‑on courses that makes sure your child learns those missing topics before they take the state test. A child works through small, focused units that each match a specific state standard, then takes short state‑style checkups so you know they’re ready. In our first group of states, this meant adding about 20 short topic units for a Texas third grader, but only 3 for a Massachusetts third grader — because that’s all those states were actually missing. For most students, the supplement adds roughly 8-10 hours of focused practice alongside their main math course to fully cover their state’s expectations.
status.standards_covered: Covers the measured residual of each state's G3 standards vs CCSS G3: TEKS 20, FL B.E.S.T. 19, MA 3, AZ 4, CA 0 (verbatim adopter). NOT covered: anything the national course teaches; state process standards (carried by test-form item design); below-grade content; empty cells stay empty by measurement.
status.passes_test: Crush, not pass: STAAR Masters (TX), FAST Level 5 (FL), MCAS Exceeding (MA), AASA Highly Proficient (AZ), plus MAP 2X growth all states. Replaces: nothing gated state-only content last year — the delta was untaught and untested. National test stays the grade gate; state test becomes a byproduct.
status.entry_gate: National grade-level gate (or concurrent enrollment) at 90%. Supplement is delta-only; never re-teaches national content. Exit: Form A/B/C at 90%, rotated on retake.
status.xp_hours: Sized by measurement, not capped. Knows-nothing: TX ≈ 11–12 XP hrs (53 lessons), FL ≈ 12 (56), MA ≈ 1.5–3 (13), AZ ≈ 2–4 (16), CA 0. Knows-all ≈ under 1 hr (diagnostic + form). Median pending tester pacing (2 enrolled). 1 XP/focused-min at ≥80%. Farmability: XP on first-attempt success only; quizzes formative; mastery only via A/B/C forms with disjoint, re-verified items.
status.effective_for: Students past the national G3 gate at TX/FL/AZ/MA campuses ahead of state test windows, esp. voucher/ESA families. Named roster: 5–20/state from TimeBack Reporting [pull pending]. Per-student 1–2wk hypothesis: MA/AZ complete supplement ≤10 school days + ≥90% Form A; TX/FL ≥50% units at pace + on-track; all show pre→post gain on gap-standard items.

In the pilot, 4 of 5 students passed the Texas end-of-course math test on their first try, averaging 94% — one scored a perfect 100. The typical student finishes a full grade of math in about 22 hours of focused work; students who already know the material place out in 2-6 hours.

status.name: AlphaPhonics Assessments (testing app)
status.subject: Reading
status.replaces: Literably screeners in the PK-2 Reading sequence. Confirmed direction in Worksmart: Acadience-inspired ORF + Nonsense-Word Fluency with AI-graded audio scoring plus a retell comprehension score (5/31); 4 Acadience-test MVPs complete (6/21); student testing of AI-grading accuracy planned (6/29); screener grading improvements (7/13).
status.hole_filling: At this time we are not proposing to change hole filling logic from the previous school year. Like before, students who don't pass an assessment may be placed in the hole filling course.
status.parent_summary: You don't need to take our word that the courses are strong. We collect robust data to let the parents (and ourselves) verify that the courses drive learning outcomes. We have taken inspiration from the most evidence-backed assessment suites to design our assessments. When it comes to early literacy and phonics, some of the best metrics you can look at are proficiency in decoding nonsense words (so we guarantee that students haven't memorized those words) and oral reading fluency to know how accurately and quickly they're reading. A fluent reader is set up for success as they go onto other courses.
status.standards_covered: Our assessment suite covers Common Core K-2 Reading Foundational Skills. However, some phonological awareness standards are explicitly excluded because the learning scientists have determined they are bad pedagogically.
status.passes_test: All students will reach 104 words per minute on Acadience Grade 2 passages, which Acadience defines as above the benchmark. "Students above the benchmark are highly likely to achieve important reading outcomes."
status.entry_gate:
These assessments ARE the gates that determine what courses a student tests out of.
To test out of K: NWF 40+ CLS · 4+ WWR
To test out of 1: ORF 67 wpm
To test out of 2: ORF 104 wpm

NWF = nonsense word fluency
CLS = close letter sounds
WWR = whole words read
ORF = oral reading fluency
status.xp_hours: The assessments are infrequent, and not like a normal course. Students will earn 2 XP for taking an assessment.
status.effective_for: PK-2 early readers needing placement/progress screening; the two most predictive early-literacy measures in the research literature (ORF, NWF). Our 2-week hypothesis (or even sooner) is that we will manually comb through some data points to ensure the AI is grading things as accurately as possible, and even add some to our eval. Accurate AI makes for the best quality assessment.

status.name: Academics Science
status.subject: Science
status.replaces:
Current G3-8 Science main sequence — see https://timeback-loops-k8.vercel.app/course-sequence

Where it lands: Replaces the Grades 3-8 Science main-sequence courses
status.hole_filling: The courses themselves are the base course, but they also double as the source for hole-filling
status.supplements: Contains. The multi-state coverage is built into the base course — it teaches the extra topics states like Texas and Florida add on top of the national standards, so no separate state-supplement course is needed. The few remaining state-specific gaps (closed-loop circuits at G4–5; acids/bases, refraction, tides, and atmospheric composition at MS) are being folded directly into the base courses rather than shipped as standalone supplements — a handful of lessons doesn't warrant its own course.
status.parent_summary: Our science courses (grades 3–12) replace the learning programs we used last year. Each course teaches everything your child is expected to know in science at their grade — and, because states differ, it also covers the extra topics that states like Texas, Florida, and New York add to their own tests, including hands-on engineering. The courses are focused and stay right at your child's grade level, so a full year of science takes about 12–18 hours of real work instead of the 25-plus hours the old program stretched it across. Best of all, a child can't just click through: a lesson only counts when they answer the questions correctly on their own, and they keep practicing until they've genuinely mastered the topic. When a child finishes a grade and passes our final test for it at 90%, they should earn the highest score level on their state's science test and place in the top 10% of students nationwide — and for the many kids who've been stuck repeating a grade, the course targets exactly what they're missing, with progress you can see in about two weeks.

status.parent_summary: [AI guess — verify — Playcademy Vocabulon BrainLift, Purpose + SPOV 7] Last year your child practiced vocabulary in VocabLoco, which rewarded clicking through activities — points could pile up without the words sticking. This year Vocabulon replaces it in 3rd grade. One tap starts a five-minute session, about twice a day. The app teaches each new word first — a kid-friendly definition, the word read aloud, real examples — before it ever quizzes on it. Your child types each word out instead of picking from choices, every wrong answer gets an explanation of why, and each word keeps coming back for review right before it would be forgotten, until it is truly known. Prediction: a typical 3rd grader masters the full grade word list in roughly 10-12 weeks of ten-minute days, and that mastery shows up as stronger reading-comprehension scores (measured on the ITBS vocabulary section and MAP).
status.standards_covered: [AI guess — verify — Playcademy Vocabulon BrainLift, CCSS section] CCSS Language: Vocabulary Acquisition and Use (CCRA.L.4-6), grades 3-6. L.4a/c/d — meaning from sentence context, reference-style definitions, verifying meaning in context (flashcard definitions, example-selection, fill-in-the-blank). L.5b/c — correct-vs-incorrect real-world usage and shades of meaning (unlocked only once a word is secure, per the interference principle). L.6 — grade-appropriate Tier-2 academic words including accurate spelling and productive use (typed spelling, sentence-writing, paraphrasing). Deliberately not delivered yet: L.4b morphology/affixes — the data model supports affixes and the system shadow-detects morpheme-region spelling errors to size the need, but no morphology lessons are taught today.
status.passes_test: [AI guess — verify — Playcademy Vocabulon BrainLift, Tests + Pilot] Prediction: significantly improved MAP reading-comprehension scores relative to students who took VocabLoco; the app-level contribution measure is the ITBS (Iowa Test of Basic Skills) vocabulary section — no numeric threshold or vs-replaced delta stated in the BrainLift yet. Internal mastery bar: a word is mastered at >=30-day spaced-repetition stability on both spelling and meaning; a course completes at 90% of words mastered or by test-out. Pilot support (20 students, 301 sessions): 90% of sessions above 80% accuracy; retention holds flat across spaced reviews; ~131 sessions projected to complete a course vs the 96-120 target.

status.parent_summary: [AI guess — verify] Nothing changes for most students: Membean stays the main vocabulary course for grades 7-12. What changes is what happens when a student fails a vocabulary test. Until now they were sent back to open-ended review they could satisfy just by putting in time — some students have been stuck in that loop for months. Now they get a course built from the exact words they missed: a short lesson for each word, then practice sets they must pass at 80% — and every retry uses fresh questions, so the only way through is to actually know the words. A full course is about 20 focused hours, and most students are assigned only the few word lists they actually need. (Source: owner's new Parent Summary block in the Vocabulary BrainLift, read 2026-07-22.)
status.standards_covered: [AI guess — verify] Covers CCSS vocabulary strands: L.x.4-6 (Vocabulary Acquisition & Use — context, affixes/roots, figurative language, nuance, academic/domain vocabulary), RL/RI.x.4 (word meaning in context), W.x.2.d / W.x.3.d (precise language in writing). State crosswalk: TX (TEKS), FL (B.E.S.T.), VA (SOL) mapped individually as genuinely distinct; MA/CO/AZ/CA/GA/NY CCSS-derived (notation differences only). NOT covered (owner-stated exclusions): grammar & conventions L.x.1-2 (owned by the separate Language HF courses), reading comprehension beyond word-level meaning, writing composition as a whole (only the precise-language sub-standards), Speaking & Listening, Reading Foundational skills, literary/figurative interpretation beyond word-level meaning, and non-vocabulary state content. (Source: Barbara's BrainLift, read 2026-07-22.)

Honest caveat in BrainLift: MAP reports no standalone vocabulary subscore, so a clean isolated-impact number may not be achievable. (Source: Barbara's BrainLift, read 2026-07-22.)

status.xp_hours: [AI guess — verify] Full course 1,200 XP (15 wordlists × 80 XP: six practices at 5/10/15/15/10/5 = 60 XP plus 20 one-XP word quick-checks); 1 XP per focused minute (≈20 XP-hours full course); knows-nothing/doom-loop full-course case 1,800 XP (~30 h, 120 XP/wordlist) — full-course placement for the 3+-attempt cohort because Membean can neither target the specific missed words nor hard-gate completion. Cannot be farmed: PowerPath-100 gates XP behind the ≥80% mastery gate on each scored practice; a redo below the gate draws fresh questions from an over-provisioned, difficulty-stratified pool (~28 items/word, 600 served questions per wordlist); keys are position-balanced, distractors misconception-tied, and items never restate the definition. status.name: ISEE + SAT Vocabulary
status.subject: Vocabulary
status.replaces:
[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 'what is changing' section + SAT BrainLift, 2026-07-22] Adds the test-format layer last year's ISEE prep explicitly lacked; deliberately NOT a Membean replacement. Prior phases: a Quizlet + Edulastic hybrid (uniform lists for all students, a word never resurfaced after its single review week), then Membean (adaptive breadth with real gains, but its named structural limit was no ISEE-format simulation). This year Membean keeps breadth coverage while Alpha Vocabulary adds deep retention of the curated list in the exam's own two item formats plus spelling/production.

Where it lands: after Grade 8 Standard Vocabulary on the ISEE Upper Level path (base + diagnostic Hole-Filling companion). SAT course: net-new — the SAT BrainLift states there is no prior-year SAT-vocabulary product to compare against; placement TBD.
status.hole_filling: [AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23] ISEE: a dedicated Hole-Filling companion course (spec confirmed 2026-07-22, build pending) mirrors the base course's 39 units 1:1 by name and order, plus a 'General ISEE Vocabulary' catch-all. Trigger is diagnostic, not self-enrollment: missing a word on any of the 9 rotating AlphaTest v2 practice tests assigns that word's specific unit(s), over-assigning rather than under-assigning on ambiguous misses. 3 steps per unit (10 XP): Learn It! (reused cards optional, up to 5 new cards mandatory), Vocabulary Practice (medium difficulty, easy-tell paronym distractor slot dropped), Final Review (full test-grade, items authored disjoint from the scored AlphaTest pool). Connect It! and Spell & Learn are deliberately dropped — the learning-science rationale is now explicit (Megan's multiple-representations principle): Spell & Learn is the only exercise building a word's written/productive leg, preserved in the base course, dropped from HF because HF drills toward the exam while the base course owns building the word's full multi-format representation. Difficulty ramps a fixed four-checkpoint ladder: scaffolded base items → HF Vocabulary Practice (medium) → HF Final Review (full test-grade) → the real AlphaTest (test-grade, entirely novel items). Most students loop test → HF → retest rather than completing the base course sequentially, so HF is core curriculum delivery for a meaningful share of students, built with base-course rigor. SAT course: hole-filling not yet specified.
status.supplements: [AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23] Yes, by design — the course is one layer of a four-part system and deliberately NOT a complete solution: Membean stays as the adaptive breadth layer (ERB draws ~80-85% of real ISEE vocabulary from outside any fixed list on every form, which no fixed-list course can cover), AlphaTest v2 provides diagnostic routing into Hole-Filling, and Build the Word/morpheme units teach deriving genuinely unseen words. The course contains no internal supplements of its own; the Membean/Blitz daily-XP top-up formula is a separate allocation not yet reconciled with the course's 15-XP daily pacing (see bottleneck).
status.parent_summary: [AI guess — verify — both paragraphs verbatim from owner-authored Parent Summary nodes: ISEE BrainLift + SAT BrainLift, read 2026-07-22] ISEE course: Once the student finishes the standard vocabulary program through 8th grade, they move into a course built specifically to prepare for the Verbal Reasoning section of the ISEE Upper Level test — the vocabulary section used for admission to grades 9 through 12. Each new word is taught directly, then practiced several different ways: picking its meaning, spelling it correctly, and choosing it in the same fill-in-the-blank sentence format the real test uses. Older words keep coming back for review throughout the course, so a word learned early on is still expected to be remembered months later, not just once and forgotten. If the student misses a word on one of our practice tests, they'll get a short, targeted review of just that word bank rather than repeating material they already know. This course works alongside Membean, which students already use for standard Vocabulary courses — Membean covers the much wider range of words a real ISEE test can draw from, while this course builds deep, test-format mastery of the specific words we know matter most. The goal: a Stanine 7 or higher on the ISEE's Verbal Reasoning section — the same score band shown on the ISEE's own report to families. SAT course: This course teaches the vocabulary skills tested on the redesigned digital SAT's Reading & Writing section — specifically, questions that ask what a word means in a short passage, or which word best completes a sentence. Rather than handing students a generic list of "1,000 SAT words" to memorize, the word list was built by studying real SAT questions directly, then checked against a well-known SAT vocabulary list to make sure nothing important was missed. The hardest and most commonly-tested words in each unit get direct, dedicated instruction; the rest are still tested and explained in detail, so a student is never left guessing why an answer is right or wrong. Older words keep resurfacing throughout the course so they're not forgotten after one unit. This course covers only the vocabulary-in-context portion of the Reading & Writing section — it doesn't cover grammar, essay structure, or general reading comprehension, which are handled elsewhere.
status.standards_covered: [AI guess — verify — ISEE + SAT BrainLifts 2026-07-22] ISEE course — covered: ISEE-format familiarity + deep retention of the program's ~495 curated words (the ~300 TI-300 list + ~195 more via Hole-Filling), practiced in the real test's two Verbal Reasoning item types (Synonyms, Sentence Completion). Not covered by design: the ~80-85% of real ISEE vocabulary ERB draws from outside any fixed list (handled program-side by Membean's adaptive coverage + Build the Word morpheme derivation); CCSS or any other 3rd-party ELA standard; the ISEE's other three sections. SAT course — covered: College Board's Craft and Structure domain, the Words in Context skill only (~9% of Reading & Writing). Not covered: Information and Ideas (26%), the other 2/3 of Craft and Structure (Text Structure/Purpose, Cross-Text Connections), Standard English Conventions (26%), Expression of Ideas (20%); not CCSS-mapped — built directly to College Board's own domain/skill breakdown.
status.passes_test: [AI guess — verify — ISEE + SAT BrainLifts 2026-07-22] ISEE: ISEE Upper Level Verbal Reasoning; official Blitz Program 2025-26 goals set by Chris Locke — stanine 6-7 (Non-Honors track), stanine 8-9 (Honors track); the owner's parent summary states stanine 7+. Explicitly a full 3-system program target (Alpha Vocabulary + Membean + AlphaTest) — the course's isolated contribution is unmeasurable by design. Comparison vs last year now documented: the Membean-only phase logged Benny +55, Zayen +51, Jacob +8 percentile points; Austin's completed Blitz cohort (Sep-Dec 2025) averaged +29 percentile points, hitting the Non-Honors goal for 10 of 14 and Honors for 2 of 14. SAT: no scaled-score threshold (depends on interaction with the other R&W strands); stated aim = 100% accuracy on Words in Context items in R&W Module 1, banking errors for the other 28 items and routing into the harder, uncapped-score Module 2.
status.entry_gate: [AI guess — verify — ISEE + SAT BrainLifts 2026-07-22] ISEE base course: testing out of Grade 8 Standard Vocabulary. ISEE Hole-Filling: no separate entry test — placement is automatic and diagnostic; missing a word on any of the 9 rotating AlphaTest v2 practice tests triggers assignment of that word's specific unit(s). SAT course: explicitly TBD — no diagnostic placement rule designed yet.
status.xp_hours: [AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23 + SAT BrainLift 2026-07-22] ISEE base course: 820 XP ≈ 13.7 h (31 thematic units × 20 XP + 8 morpheme units × 25 XP) — same for median, knows-it-all, and passed-entry-knows-nothing profiles (no per-unit test-out; knows-it-all fast path flagged as an open design question). Per-unit pricing explicit: 20 XP base / 10 XP Hole-Filling. ISEE Hole-Filling ceiling: 400 XP ≈ 6.7 h worst case, scoped to only the words a student actually missed (median TBD until build). SAT base course: 1,125 XP ≈ 18.75 h (75 units × 15 XP; 15 XP ≈ 15 min per unit); the per-profile splits (median / already-knows / passed-entry-knows-nothing) are explicitly TBD until unit-by-unit build starts. 1 XP = 1 focused minute across both courses — confirmed on the ISEE side by the real designed daily pace: one unit's core grind (Vocabulary Practice + Spell & Learn + Final Review) runs 15 XP ≈ 15 minutes/day. Not farmable in either: Learn It! is 0 XP — no credit for exposure; every graded step requires a specific correct response (Connect It! word-definition pairs; typed Spell & Learn — the hardest format to guess into; grammar- and collocation-matched plausible distractors, not obviously-wrong fillers); Final Review runs fixed-order rather than retry-until-correct; HF review items authored disjoint from the scored AlphaTest pool.
status.effective_for: [AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23 + SAT BrainLift 2026-07-22] ISEE: students on the ISEE Upper Level path — applicants to grades 9-12 — entering immediately after completing Grade 8 Standard Vocabulary (base course); students who miss vocabulary items on an AlphaTest v2 practice test (Hole-Filling). status.parent_summary: Until now Alpha had no dedicated high-school writing course. This year grades 9-12 each get a writing course that teaches the exact moves real state and college tests score — building from strong sentences to paragraphs to full essays, with support that fades as your student shows they can write unaided. Each course is roughly 15-20 focused hours, and each grade ends with a real cold-write gate: an unscaffolded essay on a source the student has not seen before. By grade 12 your student is practicing full, unscaffolded essays at the college-ready bar. (source: owner BrainLift, read 2026-07-21)

brainlift.insight: Academic Projects replace existing app/course cells in the PowerPath course sequence with apps/courses that deliver better academic outcomes. This database is the agreed master data source for all of them.

The north-star question: what are the changes to the course sequence for the 2026-2027 school year that will be ready for students on August 1st? Answer = sy2026_27_changes (projects) + stack_changes (per-cell now→next diff).

Gaps are the product. Publishing missing/incorrect data to each subject owner and Learning Science sponsor is how it gets fixed — owners and sponsors fix the data themselves, no project managers tracking anyone down.
Delivering for students is its own best reward: this source highlights successes AND failures in delivering better learning outcomes. Do not soften either.

This is a raw data source, not a translated one. Frontier AI reasons under uncertainty with incomplete data better than any human — hand the calling LLM pure data, never interpretation layered on top.
The current production course sequence (what "now" means, and what projects replace) is live at https://timeback-loops-k8.vercel.app/course-sequence.

Approval flow, in order: plan_approved_by_ai → approved_by_learning_science → ready_for_students (owner) → approved_by_andy → approved_by_campus_dris → approved_by_guides. release_date is the owner's prediction of all-approved.
Improve me: file improvement_requests rows (any calling LLM or human can). They are stack-ranked and worked as the data source's own improvement loop.

Every course must answer six questions (the parent/customer summary is these answers rephrased in parent language): subject+grades covered; which 3rd-party standards it covers AND does not cover; the parent-recognizable standardized test passed at what threshold on completing the course(s) and their mastery gates; the entry mastery gate and threshold; XP hours to complete (median / knows-it-all / knows-nothing entrant, XP per focused minute, whether XP is farmable); and which students it works for — named Alpha students with 1-2 week falsifiable hypotheses.

changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23] ISEE: a dedicated Hole-Filling companion course (spec confirmed 2026-07-22, build pending) mirrors the base course's 39 units 1:1 by name and order, plus a 'General ISEE Vocabulary' catch-all. Trigger is diagnostic, not self-enrollment: missing a word on any of the 9 rotating AlphaTest v2 practice tests assigns that word's specific unit(s), over-assigning rather than under-assigning on ambiguous misses. 3 steps per unit (10 XP): Learn It! (reused cards optional, up to 5 new cards mandatory), Vocabulary Practice (medium difficulty, easy-tell paronym distractor slot dropped), Final Review (full test-grade, items authored disjoint from the scored AlphaTest pool). Connect It! and Spell & Learn are deliberately dropped — HF drills toward the exam; the base course owns building the word's full multi-format representation. Most students loop test → HF → retest rather than completing the base course sequentially, so HF is core curriculum delivery for a meaningful share of students, built with base-course rigor. SAT course: hole-filling not yet specified.

SAT course: hole-filling not yet specified.

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23] Yes, by design — the course is one layer of a four-part system and deliberately NOT a complete solution: Membean stays as the adaptive breadth layer (ERB draws ~80-85% of real ISEE vocabulary from outside any fixed list on every form, which no fixed-list course can cover), AlphaTest v2 provides diagnostic routing into Hole-Filling, and Build the Word/morpheme units teach deriving genuinely unseen words. The course contains no internal supplements of its own; the Membean/Blitz daily-XP top-up formula is a separate allocation not yet reconciled with the course's 15-XP daily pacing (see bottleneck).

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-23] ISEE: a dedicated Hole-Filling companion course (spec confirmed 2026-07-22, build pending) mirrors the base course's 39 units 1:1 by name and order, plus a 'General ISEE Vocabulary' catch-all. Trigger is diagnostic, not self-enrollment: missing a word on any of the 9 rotating AlphaTest v2 practice tests assigns that word's specific unit(s), over-assigning rather than under-assigning on ambiguous misses. 3 steps per unit (10 XP): Learn It! (reused cards optional, up to 5 new cards mandatory), Vocabulary Practice (medium difficulty, easy-tell paronym distractor slot dropped), Final Review (full test-grade, items authored disjoint from the scored AlphaTest pool). Connect It! and Spell & Learn are deliberately dropped — HF drills toward the exam; the base course owns building the word's full multi-format representation. Most students loop test → HF → retest rather than completing the base course sequentially, so HF is core curriculum delivery for a meaningful share of students, built with base-course rigor. SAT course: hole-filling not yet specified.

[AI guess — verify — Playcademy Vocabulon BrainLift, Tests + Pilot] Prediction: significantly improved MAP reading-comprehension scores relative to students who took VocabLoco; the app-level contribution measure is the ITBS (Iowa Test of Basic Skills) vocabulary section — no numeric threshold or vs-replaced delta stated in the BrainLift yet. Internal mastery bar: a word is mastered at >=30-day spaced-repetition stability on both spelling and meaning; a course completes at 90% of words mastered or by test-out. Pilot support (20 students, 301 sessions): 90% of sessions above 80% accuracy; retention holds flat across spaced reviews; ~131 sessions projected to complete a course vs the 96-120 target.

[AI guess — verify — Playcademy Vocabulon BrainLift, CCSS section] CCSS Language: Vocabulary Acquisition and Use (CCRA.L.4-6), grades 3-6. L.4a/c/d — meaning from sentence context, reference-style definitions, verifying meaning in context (flashcard definitions, example-selection, fill-in-the-blank). L.5b/c — correct-vs-incorrect real-world usage and shades of meaning (unlocked only once a word is secure, per the interference principle). L.6 — grade-appropriate Tier-2 academic words including accurate spelling and productive use (typed spelling, sentence-writing, paraphrasing). Deliberately not delivered yet: L.4b morphology/affixes — the data model supports affixes and the system shadow-detects morpheme-region spelling errors to size the need, but no morphology lessons are taught today.

[AI guess — verify — Playcademy Vocabulon BrainLift, Purpose + SPOV 7] Last year your child practiced vocabulary in VocabLoco, which rewarded clicking through activities — points could pile up without the words sticking. This year Vocabulon replaces it in 3rd grade. One tap starts a five-minute session, about twice a day. The app teaches each new word first — a kid-friendly definition, the word read aloud, real examples — before it ever quizzes on it. Your child types each word out instead of picking from choices, every wrong answer gets an explanation of why, and each word keeps coming back for review right before it would be forgotten, until it is truly known. Prediction: a typical 3rd grader masters the full grade word list in roughly 10-12 weeks of ten-minute days, and that mastery shows up as stronger reading-comprehension scores (measured on the ITBS vocabulary section and MAP).

changes.old_value: Median: ~1,370 XP ≈ 22 hours. Knows-all: 120–385 XP, 2–6 hours (diagnostics skip known material). Knows-nothing: ~2,100 XP, 28–36 hours (failed material re-serves until mastered). XP per focused minute: 0.8–1.2, median 1.0 (1 XP ≈ 1 minute). Farmable? No — idle and tab-switch detection, per-question time caps, spam-clicking fast-fails to 0 XP, low accuracy earns no bonus.
changes.new_value: TimeBack production 2026-07-22 (median totalXp/60 across active per-student course copies, per the timeback skill doctrine): "3rd Grade Math" median 1,320 XP over 13 priced copies = ~22.0 h; "4th Grade Math" median 940.5 XP over 536 priced copies = ~15.7 h. No "Blu Math"-titled course exists in the catalog; Incept serves per-student copies.

Completion defined in XP plus a required cumulative-bank mastery score — closes the old Membean log-time gate. XP totals are now consistent across the BrainLift (Q5 block, 'what is changing', and Parent Summary all say 1,200 full / 80 per wordlist). (Source: Barbara's BrainLift, read 2026-07-22.)
changes.new_value: [AI guess — verify] Full course 1,200 XP (15 wordlists × 80 XP: six practices at 5/10/15/15/10/5 = 60 XP plus 20 one-XP word quick-checks); 1 XP per focused minute (≈20 XP-hours full course); knows-nothing/doom-loop full-course case 1,800 XP (~30 h, 120 XP/wordlist) — full-course placement for the 3+-attempt cohort because Membean can neither target the specific missed words nor hard-gate completion. Cannot be farmed: PowerPath-100 gates XP behind the ≥80% mastery gate on each scored practice; a redo below the gate draws fresh questions from an over-provisioned, difficulty-stratified pool (~28 items/word, 600 served questions per wordlist); keys are position-balanced, distractors misconception-tied, and items never restate the definition. Completion also requires the cumulative-bank mastery score, not XP alone. (Source: Barbara's BrainLift, read 2026-07-22.)

changes.new_value: Entry: Grade-8 reading proficiency mastered (MAP Reading RIT at or above the grade-8 median, or a passed G8 reading gate), ability to read ~1050L, and typing (free-response with evidence citing). G10 requires passed G9 Primer, and so on up.

changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify] If testing shows your child has specific grammar or punctuation gaps — say run-on sentences or comma rules — these courses fix exactly those gaps and nothing else. Each of 342 short lessons teaches one specific standard, your child is placed by a diagnostic (no wasted review), and each lesson is passed at an 80% mastery bar. A child with few gaps is done in an hour or two; one with many might spend 15-25 hours. Progress shows up on the national MAP Language Usage test. (source: owner BrainLift, read 2026-07-21)
changes.new_value: This Language Hole-Filling course helps students in grades 3–12 close specific gaps in grammar, punctuation, and usage — the same skills measured by the MAP Language Usage test. Rather than putting every student through the same material, a diagnostic first identifies which specific skills a student is missing, and the student is then assigned only the lessons needed to close those gaps, each requiring an 80% mastery score before moving on. Mastery checkpoints include AI-graded rewrite tasks that accept any valid correct answer rather than one memorized phrase, and quizzes draw from large question pools so students can't pass by guessing or memorizing; time to finish varies by student since it depends on how many gaps they have, and success is measured not just by clearing in-course checkpoints but by whether those same skills stop showing up as gaps on a later MAP Language Usage retest.

changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify] Until now there was no real grammar-and-language course for 9th and 10th graders — students only met this material through tests and gap-fixing. This year they get a purpose-built course: every lesson teaches one language standard error-first — the student starts from a broken sentence and works out how to fix it — instruction cannot be skipped ahead of the quiz, and each lesson is passed at an 80% mastery bar. Rewrite checkpoints require students to fix sentences in their own words and are graded on meaning, so a memorised answer will not pass. Grade 9 is about 1,100 XP and Grade 10 about 1,030 — roughly 17–18 focused hours each. Progress shows on the national MAP Language Usage test. (source: owner BrainLift, re-read 2026-07-22)
changes.new_value: This Grade 9 & 10 Language course teaches grammar, punctuation, capitalization, sentence structure, and usage — the same skills measured by the MAP Language Usage test — through an error-first approach where students first spot what's wrong in a sentence and then learn the rule that fixes it, with instruction woven directly into practice so it can't be skipped. Mastery checkpoints include AI-graded rewrite tasks that accept any valid correct answer (not just one memorized phrase), and quizzes draw from large question pools, so students can't pass by guessing or memorizing; the course covers grammar and mechanics only, not vocabulary or writing composition, and success is measured not just by in-course quiz performance but by real improvement on independent test scores within about two weeks of starting.

changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify — verbatim from the owner's new Parent Summary node in the ISEE BrainLift, read 2026-07-22; the SAT course is not yet summarized] Once the student finishes the standard vocabulary program through 8th grade, they move into a course built specifically to prepare for the Verbal Reasoning section of the ISEE Upper Level test — the vocabulary section used for admission to grades 9 through 12. Each new word is taught directly, then practiced several different ways: picking its meaning, spelling it correctly, and choosing it in the same fill-in-the-blank sentence format the real test uses. Older words keep coming back for review throughout the course, so a word learned early on is still expected to be remembered months later, not just once and forgotten. If the student misses a word on one of our practice tests, they'll get a short, targeted review of just that word bank rather than repeating material they already know. This course works alongside Membean, which students already use for standard Vocabulary courses — Membean covers the much wider range of words a real ISEE test can draw from, while this course builds deep, test-format mastery of the specific words we know matter most. The goal: a Stanine 7 or higher on the ISEE's Verbal Reasoning section — the same score band shown on the ISEE's own report to families.
changes.new_value: [AI guess — verify — both paragraphs verbatim from owner-authored Parent Summary nodes: ISEE BrainLift + SAT BrainLift, read 2026-07-22] ISEE course: Once the student finishes the standard vocabulary program through 8th grade, they move into a course built specifically to prepare for the Verbal Reasoning section of the ISEE Upper Level test — the vocabulary section used for admission to grades 9 through 12. Each new word is taught directly, then practiced several different ways: picking its meaning, spelling it correctly, and choosing it in the same fill-in-the-blank sentence format the real test uses. Older words keep coming back for review throughout the course, so a word learned early on is still expected to be remembered months later, not just once and forgotten. If the student misses a word on one of our practice tests, they'll get a short, targeted review of just that word bank rather than repeating material they already know. This course works alongside Membean, which students already use for standard Vocabulary courses — Membean covers the much wider range of words a real ISEE test can draw from, while this course builds deep, test-format mastery of the specific words we know matter most. The goal: a Stanine 7 or higher on the ISEE's Verbal Reasoning section — the same score band shown on the ISEE's own report to families. SAT course: This course teaches the vocabulary skills tested on the redesigned digital SAT's Reading & Writing section — specifically, questions that ask what a word means in a short passage, or which word best completes a sentence. Rather than handing students a generic list of "1,000 SAT words" to memorize, the word list was built by studying real SAT questions directly, then checked against a well-known SAT vocabulary list to make sure nothing important was missed. The hardest and most commonly-tested words in each unit get direct, dedicated instruction; the rest are still tested and explained in detail, so a student is never left guessing why an answer is right or wrong. Older words keep resurfacing throughout the course so they're not forgotten after one unit. This course covers only the vocabulary-in-context portion of the Reading & Writing section — it doesn't cover grammar, essay structure, or general reading comprehension, which are handled elsewhere.

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 'what is changing' section, 2026-07-22] Adds the test-format layer last year's ISEE prep explicitly lacked; deliberately NOT a Membean replacement. changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify] AP science courses are moving onto Alpha's own platform this year. Instead of third-party prep with question banks that repeat (so students could memorize answers instead of learning), AP Biology and AP Chemistry get Alpha-built courses that find and fix each student's actual gaps, use fresh questions every time, and — the big one — grade the written free-response sections instantly against the real AP rubric, since that's half the exam score and where students lose the most. For AP Physics, Alpha is testing two outside apps head-to-head before committing. The target for all three: a 4 or 5 on the real AP exam. (source: owner BrainLift, read 2026-07-21)

For AP Physics, Alpha tested two outside apps head-to-head this summer: the current pick is PhysicsGraph as the main course, with AP Crush alongside for exam-style practice and instant grading of written answers — a choice that will be revisited as AP Crush finishes publishing its remaining units. changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify] Until now there was no real grammar-and-language course for 9th and 10th graders — students only met this material through tests and gap-fixing. This year they get a purpose-built course: every lesson teaches one language standard error-first — the student starts from a broken sentence and works out how to fix it — instruction cannot be skipped ahead of the quiz, and each lesson is passed at an 80% mastery bar. Rewrite checkpoints require students to fix sentences in their own words and are graded on meaning, so a memorised answer will not pass. Grade 9 is about 925 XP and Grade 10 about 850 — roughly 15 focused hours each. Progress shows on the national MAP Language Usage test. (source: owner BrainLift, re-read 2026-07-22)

changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify — from the ISEE Upper Vocabulary BrainLift 2026-07-22; the SAT course is not yet summarized] After finishing 8th-grade vocabulary, students preparing for the ISEE — the entrance exam for admission to grades 9-12 — master the roughly 500 words the exam is most likely to draw from, practicing them exactly the way the test asks: choosing synonyms and completing sentences, plus typing each word's spelling from memory so it sticks. About 14 hours of work in all, and a companion course automatically re-teaches only the specific words a student misses on practice tests. Prediction: students who complete the full vocabulary program (this course plus the Membean word-trainer and practice tests) will score Above Average or better on the ISEE verbal section — the top quarter of all test-takers, aiming for the top 10%.
changes.new_value: [AI guess — verify — verbatim from the owner's new Parent Summary node in the ISEE BrainLift, read 2026-07-22; the SAT course is not yet summarized] Once the student finishes the standard vocabulary program through 8th grade, they move into a course built specifically to prepare for the Verbal Reasoning section of the ISEE Upper Level test — the vocabulary section used for admission to grades 9 through 12. Each new word is taught directly, then practiced several different ways: picking its meaning, spelling it correctly, and choosing it in the same fill-in-the-blank sentence format the real test uses. Older words keep coming back for review throughout the course, so a word learned early on is still expected to be remembered months later, not just once and forgotten. If the student misses a word on one of our practice tests, they'll get a short, targeted review of just that word bank rather than repeating material they already know. This course works alongside Membean, which students already use for standard Vocabulary courses — Membean covers the much wider range of words a real ISEE test can draw from, while this course builds deep, test-format mastery of the specific words we know matter most. The goal: a Stanine 7 or higher on the ISEE's Verbal Reasoning section — the same score band shown on the ISEE's own report to families.

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 'what is changing' section, 2026-07-22] Adds the test-format layer last year's ISEE prep explicitly lacked; deliberately NOT a Membean replacement. Prior phases: a Quizlet + Edulastic hybrid (uniform lists for all students, a word never resurfaced after its single review week), then Membean (adaptive breadth with real gains, but its named structural limit was no ISEE-format simulation). This year Membean keeps breadth coverage while Alpha Vocabulary adds deep retention of the curated list in the exam's own two item formats plus spelling/production.

Where it lands: after Grade 8 Standard Vocabulary on the ISEE Upper Level path (base + diagnostic Hole-Filling companion); SAT course placement TBD.

changes.old_value: [AI guess — verify] Until now there was no real grammar-and-language course for 9th and 10th graders — students only met this material through tests and gap-fixing. This year they get a purpose-built course: every lesson teaches one language standard by starting from a broken sentence and fixing it (research shows this is about 3x faster than rule-first teaching), instruction cannot be skipped ahead of the quiz, and each lesson is passed at an 80% mastery bar. In the richest topics, students must rewrite a sentence and explain why the change alters its meaning — proving understanding, not memorization. Grade 9 is about 925 XP and Grade 10 about 850 — roughly 15 focused hours each. Progress shows on the national MAP Language Usage test. (source: owner BrainLift, read 2026-07-22)
changes.new_value: [AI guess — verify] Until now there was no real grammar-and-language course for 9th and 10th graders — students only met this material through tests and gap-fixing. This year they get a purpose-built course: every lesson teaches one language standard error-first — the student starts from a broken sentence and works out how to fix it — instruction cannot be skipped ahead of the quiz, and each lesson is passed at an 80% mastery bar. Rewrite checkpoints require students to fix sentences in their own words and are graded on meaning, so a memorised answer will not pass. Grade 9 is about 925 XP and Grade 10 about 850 — roughly 15 focused hours each. Progress shows on the national MAP Language Usage test. (source: owner BrainLift, re-read 2026-07-22)

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-22] ISEE course — covered: ISEE-format familiarity + deep retention of the program's ~495 curated words (the ~300 TI-300 list + ~195 more via Hole-Filling), practiced in the real test's two Verbal Reasoning item types (Synonyms, Sentence Completion). Not covered by design: the ~80-85% of real ISEE vocabulary ERB draws from outside any fixed list (handled program-side by Membean's adaptive coverage + Build the Word morpheme derivation); CCSS or any other 3rd-party ELA standard (unlike the feeder Standard Vocabulary courses); the ISEE's other three sections (Quantitative Reasoning, Reading Comprehension, Mathematics Achievement). SAT course (per 7/21 read): scoped to Digital SAT Words in Context only (~9% of Reading & Writing); explicit covered/not-covered statement still missing.

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-22] ISEE base course: 820 XP ≈ 13.7 h (31 thematic units × 20 XP + 8 morpheme units × 25 XP) — same for median, knows-it-all, and passed-entry-knows-nothing profiles (no per-unit test-out; knows-it-all fast path flagged as an open design question). Hole-Filling ceiling: 400 XP ≈ 6.7 h worst case, scoped to only the words a student actually missed (median TBD until build). 1 XP = 1 focused minute, confirmed by the designed daily pace (15 XP ≈ 15 min/day). Not farmable in either track: Learn It! is 0 XP; every scored step needs a specific correct response (typed Spell & Learn, collocation-matched plausible distractors, fixed-order Final Review; HF review items authored disjoint from the scored AlphaTest pool). SAT course: XP totals not yet stated.

[AI guess — verify — ISEE BrainLift 2026-07-22, flagged open] The Membean daily top-up formula's 10 XP floor at exactly stanine 6 pays less than the 15 XP daily unit costs — needs an explicit decision (override the floor or prorate the unit) before the Hole-Filling build locks in.
